speaker: interviewer
text: Interview held on May 9, 1995 with survivor Eva Bergmann, born Reiche. The interviewer is Barbara Traub. And the interview takes place in Jamaica New York, United States of America. And the language is in English. This is an interview held on May 9, 1995 with survivor Eva Bergmann Reiche. And the interviewer is Barbara Traub, and it takes place in Jamaica, New York, United States of America. The language is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good day, Mrs. Bergmann. I would like to state your full name, the place of your birth, and the year of your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was born in Berlin on November 16, 1916, in Berlin, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Berlin, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name was Reiche. And I had a brother who was a little older than I. He was two years older.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Hans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hans Reiche.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a given name at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your father's name, his occupation, and your mother's name, and her occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father in Berlin was a theoretical physicist. He worked with many quite famous physicists like Professor Lauer, Professor Ladenberg, Professor Einstein, and as a main physicist was also Professor Planck, Max Planck, who was the founder of the quantum theory. He worked for many years there until 1923 when we moved to Breslau because my father got a position there as a professor at the university. My mother actually didn't have any job in Berlin. She was a housekeeper, although we had a maid, and we had a cook. And she had [NON-ENGLISH] where she met every week with some other friends. And they had coffee and tea. And they were talking. And she went even swimming in summer into a very nice pool in Berlin. And she was-- we were very happy in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you remember the address where you lived in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: The original address where I was born, yes, was Friedrich Wilhelmstrasse number eight in Berlin. But I did not immigrate from that address. Much later on, I immigrated in '41 from a different.

speaker: interviewer
text: But on-- in Friedrichstrasse, could you describe the home in which you and your family lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I couldn't. I would not know anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: You do not remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone else live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Hans, my brother, and also our nurse maid. Our nurse maid-- we called her Nana-- lived with us actually, for almost 30 years. She was like a second mother to me, wonderful person, and Christian, of course. And she helped my-- she helped my mother when my brother was born in 1914, and stayed with us all during the time until actually we immigrated.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that the family moved eventually to Breslau.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Breslau, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything of that life in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Breslau, I went to the elementary school for five years. And then later, from 1923 to 1930, I went to the elementary school. And later, I went to a lyceum, where-- which I left. In-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH], which is an equal to almost end of high school here. All those, they were two more classes, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] which I did not attend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the lyceum, already I experienced some anti-Semitic remarks. And I like to tell you of one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a very good friend there. Her name was Bridget. She was Jewish. And on one of the outings, one of the students assaulted her in-- verbally so much. She said, oh, you are a very dirty, dirty Jew. I would never eat a piece of bread from you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why she said that? Because we use to exchange sandwiches. Was this habit of young children. And she said, I would never eat a piece of bread from you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she took the bread in her mouth and spitted it into the face of Bridget. This was actually beginning of where one woke up to the horrible things that much later happened. And I was horrified. I-- and I turned away from that other girl and kept to Bridget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the first time that you experienced this open anti-Semitic outbreak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. They never said anything to me because, you see, I was-- it did not have the Jewish religious classes in school. But I had protestant classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was baptized and confirmed ins spite of the fact that I was a Jew. Many of the Jews did that in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember approximately the year when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was confirmed when I was 13 years old. And-- but I did not practice any more Christian religion. Neither did my brother, nor my parents. But my father being Jewish, my mother was half Jewish, we were stamped at-- with Hitler as non-Aryans. That was just as bad as if you were completely Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though your mother was half Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Half Jewish, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --she was considered a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was also baptised and confirmed. But according to the race, which Hitler found so important, we were all definitely Jewish. And he wanted us out. You asked me about whether there were other incidents of anti-Semitic remarks. Yes. There were quite a few in that lyceum where I went, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Breslau, but never against me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of those other incidences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was aware of it. And even our teacher did not defend that. On the day when we went on that outing, to my opinion, the teacher could have said, why did you do that, just because she is Jewish? But she did not turn to Bridget, and didn't defend us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't protect you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a-- a horrible experience. After I had finished the [NON-ENGLISH]--

speaker: interviewer
text: May I just backtrack--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for a minute Mrs. Bergmann. Was there any discussion at home about the various anti-Semitic outbreaks? And are you aware if your father, at the university, encountered any such anti-Semitic remarks or outbreaks?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came home on that day after the outing, which affected me emotionally very much, I think even more than Bridget herself, I told it to my parents. And of course, they were horrified. No. My father was never, never assaulted verbally nor physically until they told him in 1933 that he had to leave the university being a Jew. And that was quite a blow for him. But he was not attacked otherwise verbally, because all of the students loved him. He was a very good physicist and a wonderful teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: His being asked to leave his position at the University in 1933. Did that have to do-- was it directly connected with the Nuremberg racial laws, which were promulgated at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us something about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can tell you that after [NON-ENGLISH], I think I mentioned I left the-- the lyceum. And I went to a so-called trade school where I learned sewing, and cooking, and general household tasks. And after one year-- this was a one and a half year training-- I was told that I have to leave because of the racial laws. I was a non-- I was a non-Aryan, and I had to leave the school, which upsetted me terribly. And I want to tell you how wonderful the girls were with whom I had the training. None of them turned against me. They were all horrified about the idea that I was non-Aryan. They never knew about, and I never had talked about it. But it upset me terribly at the time. And then after that, of course, my parents felt they wanted to go back to Berlin in the hope that my father would be able to work at the university under Kaiser Wilhelm's Institute in Berlin for a while where he knew so many of his physics professors, amongst them many Christian ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I again backtrack for a moment? What happened to your brother during this time? How did he fare in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My brother was also feeling that the situation got worse. He never was attacked either physically or-- or verbally. But he was in school not too happy. He was not a wonderful student anyway. And he got out, I think, even before me in [NON-ENGLISH]. Yeah. I went to [NON-ENGLISH]. And he wanted to be an radio engineer later on. He always was interested in radio engineering. And he later on learned that, also in Berlin, and worked in a shop with radio engineering.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you eventually left Breslau.

speaker: interviewee
text: After we eventually went to Berlin. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before we go back to Berlin, could you describe to us your lifestyle in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was wonderful. I enjoyed the time in Breslau very much. And before Hitler, during the time when I was confirmed, and-- I was very happy. We had little parties in our-- in our apartment. My mother was always wonderful in arranging parties, even little dance parties. And I remember that my brother went to dance school. And he-- I-- I remember he danced with Bridget. He liked Bridget. Now, I tell you, frankly, I do not know why I never entered the dance school. But I loved dancing school too. And as I say, we had a very smooth and happy time in Germany, actually until the time when Hitler came to power in '33, when at first little things happened, just little remarks here and there. But the first bad remark was the one in the lyceum when I--

speaker: interviewer
text: With Bridget.

speaker: interviewee
text: --heard with Bridget. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now your family is back in Berlin. What happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you-- forgive me for interrupting. Could you give us the year when you returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Was 1935. We returned to Berlin. And now a very difficult time began. Because when we came to Berlin, already we saw on many stores, Jewish stores, the big signs, do not buy here. These are Jews. They are the destroyer of-- of the government. And the benches were labeled with a big J, some of them, besides those where you could sit on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were only allowed to sit on those with a big J. And when you went out, you went with a fear. And it was a frightening experience to see the Gestapo standing in front of the Jewish stores. And-- but we did go out. And I know we went shopping. And our maid was a Christian. Later on-- that was much later on-- when we went out very little only, she was able to get extra food for us, while we had a special food card where there was a J on it where you were limited to buy food. We were limited to buy eggs, and bread, and milk because we were Jew. And we had to show this card to the grocery store.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your maid, the nanny, I assume--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nana, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was she able to get additional rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that is good that you ask me. You know, she-- at night, she went down. She knew there was a store called [NON-ENGLISH], which was a general food store. She went down there. She knew quite a lot of people there. She got extra eggs, and milk, and bread for us, and came up the back way, back stairs way up during the night. It was about 11 or 12 o'clock she came up with that. Of course, it was-- there was a great danger involved. By the way, she had to leave in '30-- after Kristallnacht, she had to leave. She was not allowed, as a Christian person, to work with Jewish people. And she stayed in a little apartment, a room nearby, so that she would be able to smuggle up food for us. She was absolutely wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [? Marta ?] [? Fromholz ?] was her name. And she was-- she came to us when my brother was born on the 28th of November 1914. And she stayed with us, actually, until the last day that we immigrated in the way of-- she came with us on the day that we immigrated to the [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a station where all the Jews had to meet. And she packed all the things for us that we were able to take along-- were not too many things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Bergmann, while you are in Berlin in the '30s, prior to Kristallnacht, what did you live from since your father was dismissed as a Jew? And could you also tell us whether you went back to your old apartment or whether you lived somewhere else? And describe the type of life you led.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, my father-- we lived from the pension that my dad had received, because he worked for quite a few years in Breslau. And he had also worked in Berlin before we had moved to Breslau. So we-- he got a pension. Actually, that was all we lived on. We moved to a different place in Berlin. That was a place where we also immigrated from, Heilbronner Strasse, which was near-- by [GERMAN]. And there my grandmother, the mother of my mother, lived with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Charlotte Ochs. And she was-- I remember I was very attached to my grandmother. She was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather, her husband, died already in 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was a widow.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a conductor of the Philharmonic Core in Berlin, quite a famous man. And we were glad that he didn't live through all that. And the mother of my mother-- of my father, died already in 19-- I think-- '27 too. And the father of my father died 1918. So they did not live at that time anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about your paternal grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paternal grand--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's mother, yes. She was a writer. She wrote books. And they were published. And she was quite an intelligent and-- person. But strangely enough, I was more attached to my other grandmother, the mother of my mother. Somehow, I felt more at ease with her. And actually, even though later on we were not able to get a visa for her, she was a one to whom I turned at times of misery, where I was so unhappy. Now, in Berlin, I was able to attend a kindergarten school. I was always glad to work with children. And I was able to attend a kindergarten school for one year, which was the so-called [NON-ENGLISH]. And after one year, I was told I could not work there anymore. It was a one and a half year training.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why couldn't you work there anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they told me as a non-Aryan, I had to leave the kindergarten school. And here, again, I had all my friends who stayed with me and kept with me and were wonderful when they heard that I was non-Aryan. There was no-- never talk about it before. And I was very unhappy, not knowing-- I wanted to work with children and babies. So I found a infants and babies clinic in Wannsee, which was a private clinic owned by a Jew. And this was a training of a one year. And I can never forget that one morning-- and I was only with Christian girls. I was the only non-Christian girl. And I'll never forget that one morning somebody knocked very wildly at the door of this clinic. And two Gestapo men were demanding to have the door open. And the owner opened the door. They threw him down on the floor and beat him terribly. We all saw that, when they beat him up. And he was carried out then to the truck, which they had outside. And we heard later on that he was deported to Auschwitz. His name was Fockenheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately the year that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was 1937. And in spite of the screams and tears of his wife, who wanted to run after him, they didn't pay any attention. It was a horrible experience. And I thought, well, this is the end of me, and they will take me out of-- but no. I was able for three and a half months to stay in that clinic and finish my state exam as a babies' and children's nurse. Mrs. Bergmann, in 1937, you were 21 years old. And you acquired a profession, that is babies' nurse. You mentioned that the clinic was-- the head of the clinic was-- A Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Mr. Fockenheim, a-- the Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain how that was possible despite the racial laws? And you also said you were the only Jewish girl, the only Jewish person there. Could you explain that to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, it was like a miracle that Mr. Fockenheim was still there. Actually, around us, you heard about deportation of so and so many friends of my parents. But Fockenheim was still there. Apparently-- I do not really know why. And they never assaulted me or asked me to leave before that. And when I left there, I was still able even to work in the Jewish hospital, learning massage and [INAUDIBLE], and physical therapy for a while. I think that was just one or two months. It was amazing that Herr Fockenheim was still there. But later on, I think right after he was taken away, things began to get much more serious. And my father, seeing that so many of his friends were already deported to Theresienstadt and Auschwitz, and Dachau and Bergen-Belsen, he resumed his efforts to get us out of Berlin. And he wrote to many friends who were already-- had already immigrated, asking them for letters of recommendation. And amongst them, one of them was Professor Einstein, who had immigrated in 1933. And Einstein wrote. And he contacted the American consulate in Berlin, and asked them to hurry the visa for us, requesting the visa, because it was really the last minute. But my parents did not get the visa until 1940, in-- in autumn 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to the autumn of 1940, could you tell us, how much did the Jewish population in Berlin know. In retrospect one knows that they were deported-- Jews were deported to Auschwitz, to Dachau, to Bergen-Belsen. But in 1937, the year that you just mentioned, was that known?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell you, I think it was known amongst many Christian circles. But I know that many people closed their eyes completely. They didn't want to know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why should we be bothered with it? We have a very wonderful life, they felt. And why should we be bothered? And I know that-- I know it from some people in-- from the grocery store, who said, oh, I know about the deportation of Jews. So what can we do about it? They had a-- a nonchalant attitude.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was terrible to hear that, because the friends of my parents, so many were already deported. And the situation became so close and so desperate that we almost didn't dare to go out anymore. Yes.
